visa_free.notes: K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026 (special administrative region)
K-ETA required; Ireland is NOT on the official K-ETA exempt list of 25 countries/regions
K-ETA required; max 90 consecutive days within 180-day window
K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026 (special administrative region)
K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026 (added to exemption list alongside Switzerland in 2026 extension)
K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026 (added to exemption list alongside Liechtenstein in 2026 extension)

K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026; multiple UK passport types listed: British Citizen, British Overseas Territories Citizen, British Overseas Citizen, British National (Overseas), British Subject, British Protected Person
K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026; special bilateral arrangement allows 6 months (180 days)
K-ETA required; 60 consecutive days, not exceeding 90 days within 180-day window
K-ETA required; 30 consecutive days, not exceeding 60 days within 180-day window

K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026. Confirmed by official MOFA Korea in Estonia notice (ee.mofa.go.kr/est-en/brd/m_28132/view.do?seq=44), which explicitly states Estonian nationals are temporarily exempt from K-ETA. Estonia is confirmed K-ETA eligible for 90 days at k-eta.go.kr. Estonia does not appear in the Switzerland-focused MOFA notice enumerating 25 countries, but the Estonia MOFA page is an authoritative Korean government source confirming the exemption independently.

e_visa.notes: Electronic visa (e-Visa) available via the official Korea Visa Portal (visa.go.kr) for eligible nationals who do not qualify for visa-free entry. Covers tourism (C-3-9), business, study, employment, medical treatment, and other purposes. Specific eligible nationalities must be checked through the official Visa Navigator tool at visa.go.kr as the full list is interactive and not enumerated as a static list. Visa issued without requiring applicant to visit a diplomatic mission abroad.

eta.notes: K-ETA (Korea Electronic Travel Authorization) required for most visa-free nationals. Application at k-eta.go.kr, fee KRW 10,000 (approx. USD 7-8). Valid 3 years or until passport expiry. Typically processed within 72 hours. As of 2026, 25 countries/regions are temporarily exempt from K-ETA through Dec 31, 2026: Japan, Thailand, Hong Kong, Singapore, Macau (Macao), USA (including Guam), Canada, UK, Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, Spain, Poland, Sweden, Finland, Norway, Belgium, Denmark, Austria, Australia, New Zealand, Taiwan, Switzerland, and Liechtenstein. Switzerland and Liechtenstein were newly added to the exemption list effective January 1, 2026. Additionally, Estonia is separately confirmed exempt by the official MOFA Korea in Estonia notice (ee.mofa.go.kr/est-en/brd/m_28132/view.do?seq=44). Ireland is NOT exempt and requires K-ETA. Persons aged 17 and under or 65 and above are also exempt from K-ETA. K-ETA holders also exempt from e-Arrival Card requirement.

visa_policy.notes: South Korea has visa-free agreements with approximately 111 countries/territories. Most visa-free nationals still require K-ETA (KRW 10,000). As of January 1, 2026, 25 countries/regions are temporarily exempt from K-ETA through Dec 31, 2026: Japan, Thailand, Hong Kong, Singapore, Macau, USA (including Guam), Canada, UK, Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, Spain, Poland, Sweden, Finland, Norway, Belgium, Denmark, Austria, Australia, New Zealand, Taiwan, Switzerland, and Liechtenstein. Switzerland and Liechtenstein were newly added to the exemption as of January 1, 2026 (previously 23 countries). Estonia is additionally confirmed K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026 by the official MOFA Korea in Estonia notice (ee.mofa.go.kr). Ireland is NOT on the K-ETA exempt list and requires K-ETA. South Korea does not operate a traditional visa-on-arrival system. The e-Arrival Card (www.e-arrivalcard.go.kr) is required for most non-K-ETA travelers and can be completed up to 3 days before arrival. Visa-free transit (B-2, up to 30 days) available for nationals of countries requiring a visa if they hold valid visas for US, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, or 32 European countries. Excluded from transit: nationals of Syria, Sudan, Iran, Cuba, Afghanistan, Iraq, Nigeria, Ghana, Egypt, Peru, Syria, Sudan, Macedonia, Kosovo, Cuba, Palestine, Iraq, Yemen, Afghanistan, Cameroon, Somalia, Gambia, Senegal, and others.

credential.subtype: any valid visa (including permanent residency; Guam and Saipan included)

conditional_access.conditions: B-2 Tourist in Transit status. Applies to nationals of countries that require a visa to enter Korea. Traveller must be transiting through Korea en route to or from the US (including Guam and Saipan), Canada, Australia, or New Zealand, or a third country. Must hold confirmed onward flight departing within 30 days. Visa must be affixed as a sticker on the passport (Australia exception: validity confirmed via VEVO system). Must not have been denied entry to Korea within past 3 years; must not have received fine of KRW 5 million+ or deportation order; must have stayed no more than 3 days in any prior transit country. Cannot enter Korea and return to the same departure country without proceeding to a different destination. Resumed April 30, 2023.

B-2 Tourist in Transit status. Applies to nationals of countries that require a visa to enter Korea. Traveller must be transiting through Korea en route to or from the US (including Guam and Saipan), Canada, Australia, or New Zealand, or a third country. Must hold confirmed onward flight departing within 30 days. Visa must be affixed as a sticker on the passport. Must not have been denied entry to Korea within past 3 years; must not have received fine of KRW 5 million+ or deportation order; must have stayed no more than 3 days in any prior transit country. Cannot enter Korea and return to the same departure country without proceeding to a different destination. Resumed April 30, 2023.

credential.subtype: any valid visa (including permanent residency; validity confirmable via VEVO system even if not sticker)

conditional_access.conditions: B-2 Tourist in Transit status. Applies to nationals of countries that require a visa to enter Korea. Traveller must be transiting through Korea en route to or from the US, Canada, Australia, or New Zealand, or a third country. Must hold confirmed onward flight departing within 30 days. For Australia: visa validity confirmed via Australian VEVO system (online.immi.gov.au); sticker form not required. Must not have been denied entry to Korea within past 3 years; must not have received fine of KRW 5 million+ or deportation order; must have stayed no more than 3 days in any prior transit country. Resumed April 30, 2023.

B-2 Tourist in Transit status. Applies to nationals of countries that require a visa to enter Korea. Traveller must be transiting through Korea en route to or from the US, Canada, Australia, or New Zealand, or a third country. Must hold confirmed onward flight departing within 30 days. Visa must be affixed as a sticker on the passport. Must not have been denied entry to Korea within past 3 years; must not have received fine of KRW 5 million+ or deportation order; must have stayed no more than 3 days in any prior transit country. Resumed April 30, 2023.

credential.subtype: any valid visa (including permanent residency) of any of 32 European countries: Greece, Netherlands, Norway, Denmark, Germany, Latvia, Romania, Luxembourg, Lithuania, Liechtenstein, Malta, Belgium, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Sweden, Switzerland, Spain, Slovakia, Slovenia, Iceland, Ireland, Estonia, United Kingdom, Austria, Italy, Czech Republic, Croatia, Portugal, Poland, France, Finland, Hungary

conditional_access.conditions: B-2 Tourist in Transit status. Applies to nationals of countries that require a visa to enter Korea. Traveller must be transiting through Korea en route to or from one of the 32 European countries, or has departed from one of those countries and transits through Korea bound for country of origin or a third country. Must hold confirmed onward flight departing within 30 days. Visa must be affixed as a sticker on the passport. Must not have been denied entry to Korea within past 3 years; must not have received fine of KRW 5 million+ or deportation order; must have stayed no more than 3 days in any prior transit country. Resumed April 30, 2023. The 32 qualifying European countries are: Greece, Netherlands, Norway, Denmark, Germany, Latvia, Romania, Luxembourg, Lithuania, Liechtenstein, Malta, Belgium, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Sweden, Switzerland, Spain, Slovakia, Slovenia, Iceland, Ireland, Estonia, United Kingdom, Austria, Italy, Czech Republic, Croatia, Portugal, Poland, France, Finland, Hungary.

cbi.notes: South Korea does not offer a Citizenship by Investment (CBI) program. Investment programs (F-2-8 real estate, F-2-12 public fund) lead to long-term residency (F-2) and eventually permanent residency (F-5) but not direct citizenship. Naturalization after F-5 permanent residency is possible but requires Korean language proficiency and cultural knowledge testing. South Korea generally prohibits dual citizenship. No official government source confirms any direct pathway to citizenship through investment alone.

programs.name: Immigrant Investor Scheme for Real Estate (IISRE) - F-2-8 Visa
programs.notes: Investment in government-designated tourism and leisure facilities in areas such as Jeju Island, Incheon Free Economic Zone (Songdo, Yeongjong, Cheongna), Alpensia resort (Pyeongchang), Haeundae (Busan), and other designated zones. Minimum KRW 1 billion (increased from KRW 500 million in May 2023). Grants F-2 residence visa to investor, spouse, and unmarried children. After 5 years of maintaining investment, eligible to apply for F-5 permanent residency. CAUTION: Program was valid through April 30, 2026 per IFEZ official page; no official confirmation of extension found as of June 2026. Travelers should confirm current status with Korea Immigration Service before applying.

programs.name: Immigrant Investor Scheme for Public Business (IISPB) - F-2-12 Visa
programs.notes: Investment in public interest funds designated by the Minister of Justice. Minimum KRW 1.5 billion (threefold increase from prior threshold). Managed through Korea Development Bank. Grants F-2 residence to investor, spouse, and unmarried children. After 5 years of maintaining investment, eligible for F-5 permanent residency. Preliminary investment screening requires personal visit to Korea at least once.

programs.name: Points-Based Resident Visa - F-2-7
programs.notes: Non-investment points-based residency for highly skilled professionals. Requires 80+ points out of 135 based on age, education, income, Korean language ability, and residence history. Minimum 1 year of qualifying employment/residence to apply. F-5-16 permanent residency available after 3 years on F-2-7. Not an investment visa but a key highly-skilled residency pathway.

programs.name: Digital Nomad (Workcation) Visa - F-1-D
programs.eligibility: Foreign nationals who are self-employed or employed by an overseas company, working remotely, with at least 1 year of work experience in the same industry, aged 18+. Minimum income approximately KRW 88.1 million/year (double South Korea's GNI per capita). Must hold valid private health insurance (minimum KRW 100 million coverage).
programs.notes: Launched January 2024, made permanent program in 2025. Authorized stay up to 1 year, renewable once (maximum 2 years total). Dependents (spouse and minor children) eligible. Local employment in Korea prohibited. As of 2026, in-country status change from B-1/B-2 (tourist) to F-1-D permitted without leaving Korea if all eligibility requirements met.

programs.name: Top-Tier Visa (F-2 Top Talent)
programs.eligibility: World-leading talent in core future industries: semiconductors, AI, robotics, biotechnology, aerospace, defense, secondary batteries, displays. Expanded March 2026 to include STEM professors and researchers. Requires annual salary of at least 3x GNI per capita (approx. KRW 130-149 million). Must hold master's/doctorate from world top-100 university with 8+ years experience including 3+ at a global top-500 company, or equivalent research credentials.
programs.processing_time: Priority processing at dedicated centers at Seoul Immigration Office; experts in national strategic technologies processed at twice standard speed
programs.notes: Part of South Korea's 2026 Immigration Policy Strategy released March 5, 2026. Grants F-2 long-term residency with free work activities. Path to F-5 permanent residency after 3 years. Benefits include 50% income tax reduction up to 10 years, special school admission for children, and expanded housing loans.

programs.name: K-STAR Visa (F-2-7S) - Korea-Science and Technology Advanced Human-Resources
programs.eligibility: Outstanding foreign master's and doctoral degree holders in science and technology, recommended as 'excellent talent' by their Korean university. Enables F-2 residency application without prior employment offer.
programs.notes: Launched as part of 2026 immigration reforms. Cuts period-of-stay requirement for permanent residency (F-5) from 6 years to 3 years. Nationwide implementation in 2026 after pilot in 2023. Information session held at Seoul National University on April 27, 2026.

programs.name: Startup Korea Special Visa - D-8-4
programs.eligibility: Foreign entrepreneurs intending to establish a technology-based startup in South Korea. Must score at least 60-80 points under Korea's Startup Immigration Points System. Must hold bachelor's degree or higher. Physical office address required (virtual offices not accepted). Minimum capital KRW 10 million.
programs.notes: Startup Korea Special Visa (D-8-4S) launched November 2024; expanded August 2025 to allow recommendation letters from local governments and accredited accelerators in addition to the Ministry. Typical pathway: OASIS certificate (startup evaluation) -> D-10-2 startup preparation visa -> D-8-4 startup visa. Jointly administered by Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS) and Ministry of Justice (MOJ).

programs.name: E-7-M Visa (K-CORE - Korea College-to-Regional Employment)
programs.eligibility: Foreign graduates of South Korean technical/vocational colleges in manufacturing fields, employed in designated population-decline regions.
programs.notes: Introduced as part of South Korea's 2026 Immigration Policy Strategy. Allows F-2 visa application after just 3 years of continuous service in a designated population-decline region, bypassing the standard longer timeline.

outbound_official.notes: Source is the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Overseas Safety Travel portal (0404.go.kr), the 사증면제 (visa exemption) page - South Korea's official outbound consular-services reference for its citizens. Page dated March 2025. The visa_free list reflects all countries where Korean ordinary-passport holders may enter without obtaining a prior consular visa, per bilateral agreement or unilateral arrangement. Several visa-free entries still require an Electronic Travel Authorization (ETA) at destination: USA (ESTA), Canada (eTA), Australia (ETA), New Zealand (NZeTA). Indonesia and Vanuatu are listed separately as visa-on-arrival. Note: some destinations appearing in this outbound list (e.g. Afghanistan, Syria, Iran, Iraq, Libya, Yemen, Sudan, Somalia, Mali, Niger) are subject to South Korean government travel bans or advisories; Korean citizens are legally prohibited from visiting certain of these countries under the Overseas Travel Act.

validation_notes: CORRECTION (2026-06-13): Switzerland (CHE) changed from 'K-ETA required' to 'K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026'. Switzerland was newly added to the K-ETA exemption list alongside Liechtenstein effective January 1, 2026. Source: official MOFA notice at overseas.mofa.go.kr/ch-en/brd/m_23429/view.do?seq=22&page=1.

CORRECTION (2026-06-13): Liechtenstein (LIE) changed from 'K-ETA required' to 'K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026'. Liechtenstein was newly added to the K-ETA exemption list alongside Switzerland effective January 1, 2026. Source: official MOFA notice at overseas.mofa.go.kr/ch-en/brd/m_23429/view.do?seq=22&page=1.

CORRECTION (2026-06-13): Ireland (IRL) corrected from 'K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026' to 'K-ETA required'. Ireland does not appear on the official 25-country/region K-ETA exempt list per the MOFA Switzerland embassy notice. The visitkorea.or.kr official page (updated April 21, 2026) also does not list Ireland among exempt nations.

CORRECTION (2026-06-13): K-ETA exempt country count updated from '22 countries' to '25 countries/regions' in eta notes and policy notes, reflecting addition of Switzerland, Liechtenstein, and Taiwan (Taiwan was already in visa_free with exempt note but missing from the master count).
CORRECTION (2026-06-13): IISRE F-2-8 RBI program notes updated to flag that program expiry was April 30, 2026 per ifez.go.kr; no official extension confirmed as of June 2026.

CORRECTION (2026-06-13): Source URLs for K-ETA exempt countries updated to point to official MOFA Switzerland notice (overseas.mofa.go.kr/ch-en) which is the most comprehensive official enumeration of all 25 exempt countries/regions found.

ADDITION (2026-06-27): Estonia (EST) added to visa_free list as K-ETA required, 90 days. Estonia was confirmed K-ETA eligible (90 days) per k-eta.go.kr official eligibility page and was missing from the visa_free list entirely. Classified as K-ETA required (not exempt) because Estonia does not appear in the authoritative 25-country MOFA Switzerland enumeration, despite the MOFA Korea in Estonia issuing a K-ETA exemption extension notice (ambiguity flagged in gaps). Source: https://www.k-eta.go.kr/portal/guide/viewetaalification.do

CORRECTION (2026-06-29): Estonia (EST) reclassified from 'K-ETA required' to 'K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026'. The official MOFA Korea in Estonia page (ee.mofa.go.kr/est-en/brd/m_28132/view.do?seq=44) explicitly states Estonian nationals are temporarily exempt from K-ETA through December 31, 2026 KST. This is an authoritative Korean government domain. Estonia's absence from the Switzerland MOFA enumeration of 25 countries is explained by that notice focusing on the addition of Switzerland and Liechtenstein; Estonia was already exempt in prior extensions. Source URL updated to ee.mofa.go.kr notice.

VERIFIED (2026-06-29): Core 25-country K-ETA exempt list re-confirmed against MOFA Switzerland notice (overseas.mofa.go.kr/ch-en/brd/m_23429/view.do?seq=22&page=1): Japan, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore, Macao, Canada, US/Guam, UK, Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, Spain, Poland, Sweden, Finland, Norway, Belgium, Denmark, Austria, Switzerland, Liechtenstein, Australia, New Zealand - all K-ETA exempt through Dec 31, 2026.

VERIFIED (2026-06-29): Estonia K-ETA exemption confirmed from ee.mofa.go.kr official Korean embassy page; Estonia is a separately-confirmed exempt country beyond the 25 enumerated in the Switzerland MOFA notice.
VERIFIED (2026-06-27): Canada 180-day stay limit re-confirmed per MOFA Korea in Canada (mofa.go.kr/ca-en/wpge/m_5236/contents.do).
VERIFIED (2026-06-27): B-2 transit ineligible nationality list (23 countries) re-confirmed against MOFA Belgium source (mofa.go.kr/be-en/brd/m_7440/view.do?seq=748988).

VERIFIED (2026-06-29): visitkorea.or.kr page shows 23 K-ETA exempt countries (older list not yet updated to include Switzerland, Liechtenstein, and Estonia). The authoritative sources are the country-specific MOFA embassy notices (Switzerland: 25 countries, Estonia: additionally confirmed) and the ee.mofa.go.kr Estonia notice.
gaps: Complete enumerated list of e-visa eligible nationalities not extractable from visa.go.kr; requires interactive Visa Navigator tool; general availability confirmed officially.
Specific processing times for F-2-8 (IISRE) and F-2-12 (IISPB) investment visa programs not found verbatim on official pages.

IISRE (real estate investment) F-2-8 program: official ifez.go.kr page shows expiry of April 30, 2026; no official MOFA or immigration.go.kr page confirming extension found as of June 2026. Program may have lapsed or been extended - unconfirmable from currently accessible official sources.
Visa-on-arrival: South Korea does not operate a visa-on-arrival system; category is empty (confirmed).

Several visa-free countries found only in K-ETA eligible list (which is an official government domain k-eta.go.kr); the primary MFA bilateral agreements page on mofa.go.kr was inaccessible due to redirect loops.
Dual citizenship prohibition noted consistently from multiple sources but exact nationality law citation not obtained from official source.

Countries not on K-ETA eligible list (requiring standard visa application): China, Philippines, Indonesia, Bangladesh, Vietnam, Mongolia, Pakistan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Iran, Uzbekistan, Nigeria, Ghana, Egypt, Syria, Iraq, Yemen, Afghanistan, and many others - these require standard visa application and are not visa-free; their e-visa eligibility not fully enumerated.

Ghana-South Korea Visa Waiver Agreement for diplomatic and service passport holders signed June 1, 2026 (Korea-Africa Foreign Ministers Meeting, Seoul): confirmed by Ghanaian FM Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa's official X post and multiple news sources, but no official South Korean MOFA URL (mofa.go.kr press release) was accessible to confirm the rule from the Korean government side. Per official-source-first rules, not recorded in conditional_access. Once an official mofa.go.kr URL is available, this should be added as basis=passport_type, passport_types=[diplomatic, service], eligible_nationalities=[Ghana], level=visa_free.

India OCI cardholder entry to South Korea: no official South Korean government source (immigration.go.kr, visa.go.kr, mofa.go.kr) found confirming any OCI-based visa exemption or preferential entry. Not recorded.

B-2 transit program ineligible nationalities: the official list names 23 countries including 'Kirzik' (Kyrgyzstan). The consolidated list used in conditional_access entries reflects the verbatim official text. Note that as of June 2026 the B-2 program rules from the April 30, 2023 resumption notice remain in effect; no subsequent official amendment was found.
Ireland K-ETA status: visitkorea.or.kr (official, updated April 21, 2026) does not list Ireland as exempt. The prior record incorrectly listed Ireland as K-ETA exempt. Corrected to K-ETA required.

Estonia K-ETA exemption RESOLVED (2026-06-29): Previously flagged as ambiguous. Now confirmed: the MOFA Korea in Estonia notice (ee.mofa.go.kr/est-en/brd/m_28132/view.do?seq=44) explicitly confirms Estonian nationals are K-ETA exempt through December 31, 2026. Estonia has been reclassified accordingly in visa_free. Estonia does not appear in the Switzerland MOFA 25-country enumeration because that notice focuses on Switzerland and Liechtenstein as newly added countries; Estonia was already exempt in prior extensions and confirmed again for 2026.

visitkorea.or.kr K-ETA exempt list display discrepancy (2026-06-29): The official visitkorea.or.kr page shows only 23 K-ETA exempt countries/regions, appearing to omit Switzerland, Liechtenstein, and Estonia from its displayed list. Country-specific MOFA embassy notices remain the authoritative sources for the complete exempt list.

visa_types.name: Short-Term Visit Visa (C-3)
visa_types.purpose: Tourism, visiting family, short business, medical treatment, transit
visa_types.notes: Many nationalities are visa-exempt. Apply at a Korean embassy/consulate. Multiple-entry versions available. K-ETA electronic travel authorisation required for some nationalities.

visa_types.name: E-7 Specially Designated Activities Visa
visa_types.purpose: Working in a specialised job at a Korean company (requires employer sponsorship)
visa_types.notes: Requires job offer from a Korean employer. Employer must file application on behalf of the employee. Points-based system for some categories.

visa_types.name: D-2 Student Visa
visa_types.purpose: Studying at a Korean university, college, or graduate school
visa_types.notes: Requires admission letter from a Korean institution. Can work up to 20 hours/week part-time.

visa_types.name: H-1 Working Holiday Visa
visa_types.purpose: Holidaying and working in Korea (age 18–30, select nationalities)
visa_types.notes: Available for Australia, UK, Ireland, France, Germany, Canada, Japan, and others. Generally no fee or very low fee.

visa_types.name: F-1-D Digital Nomad Visa
visa_types.purpose: Remote working in South Korea for up to 2 years (launched January 2024)
visa_types.notes: Minimum annual income USD 84,600 (GNI 2x). Available to nationals from countries with visa-free or visa agreements with Korea. Cannot work for Korean employers.
